Meet With Our Experts

Minecart for Niterra

Your reps know more than your reports

Activity intelligence for the field. Your team captures what happens in the shop once, and reps, sales managers, and marketing each get the view that fits their job.

Your field team covers national accounts, dozens of WDs, and the hundreds of DC and shop locations underneath them, plus the jobbers in between. Most of that account knowledge lives in a personal spreadsheet, a notebook, or one rep's head. When a rep leaves, it leaves with them. Managers stitch together a handful of tracking sheets to see what happened last week. Marketing sees almost none of it.

Minecart fixes the capture problem first. A rep talks into their phone after a stop, the way they would tell a coworker in the parking lot. We clean it up, tie it to the account, pull out the follow-ups and any real opportunity, and route the rest to the people who need it. No call reports. No fifteen boxes to fill in. It runs next to SAP, Google Workspace, and Gemini, and it can take over note-taking and visit tracking from Sales-i.

The same field activity reaches three people, each through the lens that fits their day.

One capture, three views. The same field note serves the rep, the manager, and marketing.

The three lenses

For the field sales rep

Account memory and follow-through, without living in a CRM.

  • A pre-brief before every stop. Who you talked to last, what you promised, and the one thing worth raising today.
  • Capture by talking. A short voice memo becomes clean notes, your follow-ups, and any opportunity worth chasing.
  • A visit list that ranks itself by open quotes and fresh signals, not by how long since you last stopped in.
  • Price sheets and brand assets on the account. The Niterra material you already sent that shop, in hand instead of buried in old files.
  • Proof of WD visits from the work itself, which matters when visit volume ties to your bonus.

For the sales manager

Direct the team and hold it accountable, from one system instead of seven sheets.

  • A ranked "needs attention" queue built from real activity, so you stop reconciling spreadsheets by hand.
  • Opportunity review. Opportunities come up from the field. You confirm the real ones, dismiss the noise, set the steps to close, and track each one to the order in SAP.
  • Coverage and follow-through you can verify, with coaching signals where a rep needs support.
  • The numbers Niterra already runs on: visits made and whether they landed, who the shop calls first against Bosch and Denso, and the locations pulling ahead.
  • Continuity. The account knowledge stays when a rep moves on.

For marketing

Turn field conversations into intelligence you can act on.

  • Voice of the customer, clustered. The technical questions and objections reps hear in shops come back as patterns, not scattered notes.
  • A competitive read on first-call share, where Niterra wins and where Bosch or Denso win, drawn from real visits.
  • The content the field is asking for, the sell sheets, FAQs, and campaigns that recurring questions point straight at, in the customer's own words.
  • Asset history by account. Which Niterra material each shop already has.
  • A working link between what sales hears and what marketing builds.

How it works

The same loop, in the product. Your reps capture in the field on their phone. You and your managers see it roll up on the desktop.

In the rep's hand

Capture by talking. One voice memo after the stop, no forms.

Walk in briefed. Opportunities and risks, pulled from your own notes.

The visit list ranks itself by what needs attention.

On the show floor

Where this really earns its keep

Every booth conversation captured, and a show you can actually measure.

A show is where your team hears the most and keeps the least. Three days of booth conversations, and by the drive home most of it is gone. Minecart turns each conversation into a note the moment it happens, tied to the shop or the new lead, so none of it leaks.

  • Reps capture between handshakes. A quick voice note after each conversation, tied to the account or the new lead. No badge-scan-and-forget, no stack of cards to decode on Monday.
  • Marketing sees what actually came up. The products people asked about, the objections, and the themes, in the customer's own words.
  • Quantify what worked. Which topics and messages drew real interest, so you know where the show paid off and what to run again next year.
  • A ranked list of opportunities and prospects. Handed to sales with the context attached, not a spreadsheet of names.

At one show, a team captured about 250 notes and pulled roughly $9M in pipeline out of them. That is what turns a show you attended into a show you can measure.

On the manager's desk

The field's signal, ranked. Read the handful of notes that move the number.

Every opportunity tracked to the order. Even the losses coach the team.

Plan the whole team from one place, instead of stitching together seven spreadsheets.

What marketing sees Illustrative

Marketing gets its own lens on the same notes: where competitors are taking first call and why, the messaging that is not landing at the counter, and a ranked list of opportunities to route to sales. A sample of what one month in the field surfaces.

Where competitors are winning, and why

Denso is taking first call at import-heavy shops.

Read: counterpeople trust Denso's OE fitment on Asian nameplates. Answer it with an OE-fit and coverage proof sheet for Japanese and Korean applications, ground Niterra actually holds.

Bosch is winning at three WDs on shelf depth, not preference.

Read: this is a stocking gap. Aim a fill-rate and stocking-commitment message at the WD, not the installer.

Store-brand plugs keep winning the price-only jobs.

Read: "the cheap plug is fine" comes up a lot. Give the counter an iridium longevity and comeback-cost story they can repeat.

Messaging to update this quarter

Shops still say "NGK" and "Niterra" like two vendors.

Read: the one-company story is not landing. Lead every asset with the NGK, NTK, and Niterra relationship so the counter knows it is one line.

Installers default to whatever avoids a comeback.

Read: reframe around misfire comebacks and coil-on-plug reliability, not part specs.

Interchange and gap questions come up on every other visit.

Read: a pocket cross-reference and pre-gap guide moves more at the counter than a brand campaign.

Availability · P1

"NAPA had them on the shelf. The availability gap is costing us same-day work."

Van Wyk Auto · field note

Move: an in-stock and fill-rate message for WDs, with same-day proof.

Logistics · P2

"Add an early-afternoon delivery run and he'll make us his first call on everything."

NexGen Automotive · field note

Move: an afternoon-run first-call campaign with the WDs.

Rebate · P3

"Interested to learn more about what we offer, and our rebate program."

Hi Tech Motor · field note

Move: a field-ready rebate explainer the rep can pull up on the spot.

3 of every 5 shops Niterra slipped on named availability, not price. A stocking and message problem, not a discount problem.

Every note a rep captures sharpens the next account briefing, which sharpens the next visit, which feeds what marketing sees. The longer Minecart runs, the more your team's knowledge compounds in the system instead of leaving with the rep who knew it.
